level_2.origins_history:
Trousseau is an ancient variety native to the Jura region of eastern France, where it has been cultivated for centuries alongside Poulsard and Savagnin. DNA analysis has confirmed Savagnin as one parent, making Trousseau a half-sibling to Chenin Blanc, Sauvignon Blanc, and Grüner Veltliner.

The variety spread widely through medieval trade routes, arriving in the Iberian Peninsula where it became Bastardo (essential for Port and Madeira), in the Canary Islands as Baboso Negro, and in Galicia as Merenzao. California received Trousseau as early as 1852, making it one of the state's true heirloom varietals. By the 1885 UC Davis report, it was already established in the state. The variety nearly disappeared from France after phylloxera but has been revived by a new generation of Jura winemakers.

genetic_lineage.overview: Trousseau is a child of Savagnin, placing it in the same family as many important European varieties. The second parent remains unidentified. Its spread across Iberia and beyond reflects centuries of vineyard trade.

parents.name: Savagnin
parents.notes: The grape of Vin Jaune. DNA analysis confirms Savagnin as one parent of Trousseau.

parents.name: Unknown
parents.notes: The second parent has not been identified through DNA analysis.

offspring.name: Bastardo Magarachsky
offspring.notes: A Ukrainian crossing bred at the Magarach Institute in Crimea. Used for fortified wines.

offspring.name: Royalty
offspring.notes: A teinturier (red-fleshed) grape bred at UC Davis in 1938. Used primarily as a blending grape for color.

mutations.name: Trousseau Gris
mutations.description: A pink-skinned mutation of Trousseau, genetically identical but producing white/rosé wines. Known in California as 'Grey Riesling' despite no relation to Riesling. Thousands of acres were planted in California during the 1960s; only about 10 acres remain today. Produces light, neutral wines often used for blending.
mutations.discovery: The mutation occurred naturally. California plantings came from Jura material.

siblings.name: Chenin Blanc
siblings.notes: Shares Savagnin as a parent. The great white grape of the Loire.

siblings.name: Sauvignon Blanc
siblings.notes: Shares Savagnin as a parent. Among the world's most planted white varieties.

siblings.name: Grüner Veltliner
siblings.notes: Shares Savagnin/Traminer as a parent. Austria's signature white grape.

siblings.name: Verdelho
siblings.notes: Shares Savagnin as a parent. Important for Madeira and Australian whites.

genetic_lineage.genetic_significance: Trousseau's parentage places it in an illustrious family. The Savagnin connection helps explain its affinity for the Jura climate and its ability to produce distinctive wines under flor. Its genetic identity with Bastardo, Merenzao, and Baboso Negro across Iberia and the Atlantic islands reflects centuries of vineyard trade along medieval shipping routes.

clones.name: Clone 1004
clones.characteristics: Certified French clone. One of the primary selections used in Jura plantings.

clones.name: Clone 1017
clones.characteristics: Certified French clone. Selected for consistent quality and disease resistance.

clones.name: Clone 1021
clones.characteristics: Certified French clone. Noted for aromatic intensity.

clones.name: Clone 1026
clones.characteristics: Certified French clone. Reliable producer with good concentration.

clones.name: Clone 1114
clones.characteristics: Certified French clone. Later release with improved disease tolerance.

clones.name: Clone 1277
clones.characteristics: Certified French clone. One of the newer selections from Jura conservatory work.

clones.name: Clone 1278
clones.characteristics: Certified French clone. Companion selection to 1277.

clones.name: FPS 08
clones.characteristics: UC Davis Foundation Plant Services selection from a 19th century California vine. Pre-phylloxera heritage material representing the earliest California plantings.

clones.name: FPS 09
clones.characteristics: UC Davis selection originally catalogued as Bastardo. Confirmed as Trousseau through DNA analysis, demonstrating the variety's Iberian synonymy.

clones.name: FPS 10
clones.characteristics: UC Davis selection imported from Portugal. Represents Douro Valley Bastardo material used in Port production.

clones.name: FPS 11.1
clones.characteristics: UC Davis selection originally misidentified as Marzemino. DNA testing confirmed it as Trousseau, highlighting historical confusion around variety identification.

terroir.effect: The classic Jura soil type. Cool, clay-rich soils stress vines and produce concentrated wines with notable acidity.

susceptibilities.notes: Trousseau is highly susceptible to poor fruit set during flowering, particularly in cold or wet springs. This significantly reduces yields.
susceptibilities.impact_on_wine: Very low yields in difficult vintages

resistances.notes: Thick skins provide some natural protection against rot.

disease_profile.general_notes: Trousseau is a difficult variety to grow, with low yields and susceptibility to poor fruit set. This is one reason it nearly went extinct after phylloxera.

regional_expressions.style_summary: The heartland of Trousseau, where it produces medium-bodied reds with distinctive peppery, herbal character. Can be made in fresh, reductive styles or aged under voile (flor) for oxidative complexity. A 50-clone conservatory was planted here in 2005 to preserve genetic diversity.

typical_character.aromatics: Red cherry, pepper, herbs, earth, game

key_appellations.name: Arbois
key_appellations.style: Most Trousseau planted here, classic expression

regional_expressions.style_summary: Known as Bastardo, it is one of the authorized grapes for Port production. Contributes aromatics and freshness to Port blends. Also used for dry Douro table wines, where it adds lift and perfume.
Bastardo was historically one of the noble grapes of Madeira, producing medium-sweet styles. Nearly extinct on the island after phylloxera and oidium, with only small plantings remaining.
Known as Merenzao or Maria Ardoña, the variety is experiencing a revival in Valdeorras and Ribeira Sacra. Produces fresh, aromatic reds with marked acidity.
Known as Baboso Negro or Listán Prieto, the variety thrives on pre-phylloxera vines in volcanic soils. Produces distinctive wines with volcanic minerality and Atlantic freshness.

typical_character.aromatics: Red fruit, volcanic mineral, herbs, smoke

regional_expressions.style_summary: One of California's true heirloom varietals, arriving as early as 1852. By the 1885 UC Davis report, Trousseau was already established. Modern producers make fresh, Jura-inspired styles or use it for rosé and sparkling wines. The mutation Trousseau Gris (sold as 'Grey Riesling') was once planted to thousands of acres; only about 10 remain.
A small but growing number of Australian producers have embraced Trousseau, often sourcing from Adelaide Hills or other cool-climate sites. Wines tend toward fresh, crunchy styles.
